mappings.rationaleAI: Engineering practice covering electromagnetism, transistor technology, and infrastructure relates to machines and machine components (B) and the development of machine tools for construction and production (C).
Design, construction, and deployment of autonomous machines for manufacturing and warehouse automation directly addresses robots in computer-aided machining (D.3).
Computing technology covering core architecture, operating systems, and processing relates to computer numerical control (D.1) and computer-aided design and manufacturing (D.2).
AI applications including intelligent systems and automation connect to robots (D.3) and computer-integrated manufacturing (D.4).
Algorithms, programming, and computational complexity underpin computer numerical control (D.1) and computer-aided design and manufacturing (D.2).
Materials science studying metals, ceramics, and polymers with properties including hardness relates to the materials from which tools are made (A.1) and machine tools for shaping materials (C).
Single atom imaging, quantum confinement, and molecular machinery at the atomic scale connect to nonconventional methods of machining including electron-beam and ion beam methods (E.1).
Innovations in steam power, textile machinery, and iron production demonstrate the development of metal tools (A.1), machine mechanisms (B), and the history of machine tools (C.1).
Use of stone tools by early hominids covering Paleolithic and Neolithic periods directly addresses the early history of hand tools (A.1).
Industrial and product design at companies including Nokia, Ford, and Sony covers the integration of design with manufacturing, connecting to computer-aided design and manufacturing (D.2).
Covers fundamental structural elements including beams, bridges, and material properties, relevant to simple machines and the mechanical principles underlying machine components.

Covers the co-evolution of weapons systems and technological capabilities from prehistoric tool development through modern manufacturing, relevant to the history of hand tools and nonconventional methods of machining including laser and plasma technologies.
